I'm happy to announce Silverscript!

Silverscript is Kaspa's first high-level smart contract language. It enables DeFi, vaults, and native asset management directly on Kaspa's L1.

It's based on CashScript, but adds on top of it essential features like loops, arrays, and function calls.

It specializes in managing contracts with local state, compatible with the UTXO model, serving as a complement to vProgs (shared state).

Note: Currently supports Testnet-12 only and is experimental/subject to change.

Please try it out and give feedback!

The next PR is ready for review / comments: https://github.com/kaspanet/vprogs/pull/12

After building the L1 bridge to read data from the base layer, this PR now prepares the scheduler to links it's own artifacts (computational results and eventually proofs) to the artifacts of the L1 by associating a generic piece of BatchMetadata with each executed Batch.

This will be used by the upcoming node-framework to connect the two components in a transparent way where each produced effect can be mapped back to its source + context on the L1.

Instead of addressing executed batches purely by their index, we now address them as a combination of index + metadata - a Checkpoint which transparently maps to the subset of L2 activity contained in a ChainBlock on L1.

Another PR is ready for review / comments: https://github.com/kaspanet/vprogs/pull/13

This one introduces a coordination mechanism between rollback and pruning which are two independent processes that need to make sure that they don't interfere with each other - e.g. if we try to rollback then the pruning should pause if the rollback target is within its pruning range or the rollback should abort if the data is already gone).

In theory something like that should never happen as we only prune things that are way beyond the "point of no return" for rollbacks but to make the L2 node bullet proof, we need to handle all edge cases (even the ones that have an astronomically low chance of ever happening).

PS: I am planning to split the pruning worker into a manager and a worker to have a better separation of concerns but since this is one of the dependencies for the upcoming node-framework, I think it makes sense to merge this as soon as possible and tackle the cleanup in a separate PR after the node-framework is ready.
related note: the L1 node has subjective finality of 12h (meaning it actively rejects deep reorgs if the reorg point is below blue depth of 432,000)

Yeah I know, we should generally never see a "finalized(x), rollback(x-10)" coming from the bridge as finalized means that rollbacks beyond this point are impossible but since the L2 itself cannot really enforce that L1 behavior and we want to leave no room for non-determinism it now separately enforces this invariant.

Here is the next one: https://github.com/kaspanet/vprogs/pull/14

This PR mirrors the Checkpoint related changes, we previously introduced in the Scheduler in the L1 bridge, so both components can speak the same "language" before connecting them in the node framework.

It also introduces a ChainBlockMetadata type that contains relevant metadata about each ChainBlock that gets passed through to the L2 execution. 

It currently only contains the hash as an additional identifier and the blue score for rollback depth detection but we will add more data as it becomes necessary.

I think dropping channels is the right approach but I am not completely sure, I generally think doing something like a cleanup when channels are dropped is a bad design pattern as channel termination in many cases does not in fact signal the termination intent and can end up hiding async logic that can be very complex to understand and un**ck.

KaspaRpcClient was never designed to be used on its own and self-manage. It was always meant to have an explicit management wrapper, where the wrapper would control the lifetime and perform explicit shutdown sequences. …there are multiple reasons for this, one being notification subscriptions - any client using RPC needs explicit control over connection and disconnection as it needs to know when and how to re-subscribe to various notifications after disconnection. Hence by design KaspaRpvClient can not be used on its own. it needs a wrapper / manager with a separate task that will monitor connect/disconnect events. 

Mainly due to these considerations it was never made to be self-contained.

In GH @Max143672 pointed to GRPC integration tests, they look clean and neat but IMO they don’t address or test for one of the key requirements - a supervisor that monitors and re-subscribes to notifications needed for an application to operate.  Things get exponentially complex when this is introduced, especially in the event ordering pipelines.

Unfortunately it is not possible to automate this due to the fact that host application logic may have custom requirements when disconnection occurs. Like a wallet has to re-subscribe and then re-acquire full state from the node as it doesn’t know what may have been missed..

So I am not sure I have a clear answer. The only thing I can say is that if you venture into this, 1) make sure there id a boolean flag that enables/disables this auto-cleanup. 2) KaspaRpcClient requires an explicit shutdown management, maybe it would be better to have a wrapper that facilitates this instead of modifying KaspaRpcClient itself?. 3) are you trying to address an actual problem, I mean does this problem exist? if you address it, how do you havens subscriptions on disconnect?

TLDR; I think channel drop handling is ok as long as it is gated by a bool flag that (i guess) needs to be explicitly turned on.

I think the lack of releasing resources is awful by definition regardless of any other considerations 

And quite the opposite..if you need to keep client working - clone it somewhere and release whenever you want
i don’t disagree. :)  I think the issue also comes from the fact that it is frequently a permanent component. So that usage pattern has never exhibited itself.

Both projects are written in rust and the l1-bridge is really just a queue with denoising and pop semantics for the L2 logic. You can absolutely run this within the same process to sidestep the need for serialization / deserialization across RPC and directly work with the L1 data.

I think it would be really nice to develop some form of plugin system for the L1 node so we can accomodate for this kind of compile time modularity (I have sth similar in the L2 node implementation where the VM and the Storage are essentially compile time plugins that can be swapped in and out by just changing a tiny entry in a backend.rs).

But even then you wouldn't want to tie execution to the raw underlying L1 activity because that would force you into a form of "speculative execution" where a significant amount of computational throughput is wasted on computations and proofs that will eventually be reverted.

To avoid that you will always want to have that little overlay data structure on top that allows you to track your heuristics for efficient scheduling and its currently a struct with metadata and a prev + next pointer so a few bytes extra for each unprocessed chainblock (it's a really minimal structure that we replicate here for our heuristics).

And btw. I am not saying that we shouldn't extend the framework to reorg more efficiently - like not throwing away compute on reorg and cancel batches but pause them instead and just drain the pending tasks into some buffer that allows us to resume operations when we would reorg back and the introduction of "CheckPoints" as addresses for Batches instead of just indexes is already the first step in that direction but atm we have slightly different priorities.

But yeah, I agree - we should eventually have a fully integrated L2 node compile that side-steps the extra effort of serializing / deserializing across RPC. But that won't change the logic - only the way you retrieve data (calling a function vs calling an RPC endpoint).
This is the compile time modularity I mentioned on the L2 side, btw.
Yeah but that's about how you enqueue things in the scheduler - you don't do L2 stuff "in context" without your own worker pools and shit?

The L2 computational DAG is completely orthogonal to any of the datastructures we have on the L1. The only convergence point are the "CheckPoints / ChainBlocks" where we can transparently map one to the other but otherwise they share essentially 0 overlap?
L2 transaction is a subset of L1 transaction fields

Yeah obviously - but it's not like you can associate a proof / execution result with each transaction as the same transaction sequenced by different chainblocks will have completely different results which means that there is no longer a transparent mapping between transaction and proof / result but you instead have sth like 1 transaction could have 3 different results in different contexts and mapping and managing those contexts is what the scheduler does and that's where the magic happens.

Sure its more efficient to just read data directly from a reference than getting it from and RPC endpoint but that won't change the way you have to manage those different execution contexts and effects?

It depends on the usage pattern.  While nodes can support thousands of connections, on my experience performance of the node starts degrading when it has > 500 connections and it becomes nearly unusable > 1,000;  I am talking from PNN experience, it is heavily dependent not as much on the number of clients that connect but on the number of subscriptions and types of notifications.  Subscribing to virtual chain notifications for example will exhaust the node outbound capacity faster then monitoring few addresses.  

On PNN we are using additional RPC calls I have introduced (AFK right now), I think together with metrics (which show connections for different types of RPC (gRPC, wRPC, P2P) to dynamically balance distribution across PNN nodes.

ZK Covenant Rollup - proof-of-concept L2 on Kaspa

 It demonstrates a full deposit → transfer → withdrawal cycle on an account-based L2, with every state transition backed by a RISC Zero ZK proof and verified through on-chain covenant scripts.

  What it covers:
  - Deposits (entry via delegate script), L2 transfers, and withdrawals (exits via permission tree)
  - Both STARK (succinct) and Groth16 proof generation and on-chain verification
  - Sequence commitment that chains L1 block data into the proof
  - Sparse Merkle Tree for L2 account state
  - Full on-chain script verification through kaspa-txscript (state verification, permission, and delegate scripts)
  - CUDA-accelerated proving support

  The demo currently simulates at the transaction engine level. It builds a mock chain, generates real ZK proofs, and runs the actual covenant script verification end-to-end.

The language had some changes, and will probably have much more in the near future. I'll try to post them regularly.

## Language Changes

### Breaking
- `bytesN` syntax removed — bytes4, bytes32, etc. → byte[4], byte[32]
- `bytes` type removed — use byte[]
- `bytes()` cast removed — use byte[]()
- `sig` size is now strictly 65 bytes (was 64 or 65)
- `datasig` size is now strictly 64 bytes (was 64 or 65)
- `tx.inputs[i].lockingBytecode` → tx.inputs[i].scriptPubKey
- `tx.outputs[i].lockingBytecode` → tx.outputs[i].scriptPubKey
- `tx.inputs[i].unlockingBytecode` → tx.inputs[i].sigScript
- `tx.inputs[i].sequenceNumber` removed
- `new LockingBytecodeP2PK` → new ScriptPubKeyP2PK
- `new LockingBytecodeP2SH` → new ScriptPubKeyP2SH
- `new LockingBytecodeP2SHFromRedeemScript` → new ScriptPubKeyP2SHFromRedeemScript
- `this.activeByteCode` → this.activeScriptPubKey

### Non-Breaking
- New contract syntax: state field declarations inside the contract body (e.g. int count = initCount;)
- New literal syntax: state object { field: expr, ... }
- New built-in: `validateOutputState(int outputIndex, object newState)`
- New built-in: `readInputState(int inputIndex)` with destructured binding syntax
- Fixed-size array `.length` resolved at compile time

---

## AST Changes

### Breaking
- ParamAst.type_name: String → ParamAst.type_ref: TypeRef, where TypeRef is a new structured type:
  - TypeRef { base: TypeBase, array_dims: Vec<ArrayDim> }
  - TypeBase enum: int | bool | string | pubkey | sig | datasig | byte
  - ArrayDim: dynamic | fixed(N) | constant(name)
- FunctionAst.return_types: Vec<String> → Vec<TypeRef>
- ContractAst gains a new `fields` array (empty arrays omitted in JSON serialization)

### Non-Breaking
- New node: `ContractFieldAst` { type_ref, name, expr }
- New expression variant: `Expr::StateObject(Vec<StateFieldExpr>)`
- New statement variant: `StateFunctionCallAssign { bindings: Vec<StateBindingAst>, name, args }`
- New types: `StateFieldExpr` { name, expr }, `StateBindingAst` { field_name, type_ref, name }

So I'd like to gather thoughts again - how do you guys feel about adapting monero's adaptive blocksize?

I'll give a rough overview as I understand it:

let M_eff be the median blocksize of the last X blocks.
A block can only be as big as twice that amount.

Further, you get penalized (quadratically) for any block larger than that median, by the respective portion of the block reward being burned.

Now if we are to adopt this (a) burn should mean redistribute or at the very least forwarded to the merger.
(b) we should have some sort of a recourse for big blocks. Maybe the simplest is allowing blocks  of any size but just not giving them  any block reward from 2M_eff.
I'll prestate saying - I have my reservations regarding this scheme, based on the usual objections to de facto onchain governance.
But this has the advantage of being a tested solution already, which to my knowledge has functioned ok in practice.
@hashdag I know we discussed solutions in this line in the past, but I cannot immediately recall what or were not were your objections and what was their precise nature.
(Prconvo credits to bitcat and rossku btw, maybe we should have them here)
What's the penalty?
so 0 at 2M
yes, needless to say this is something we can play around with

this is specific to coinbase reward, but fees remain as is, correct? 
(I'm assuming that's how miners are motivated to increase capacity eventually)
sure, let's add them

basically you will only increase blocksize if fees really make up for the incurred loss...
I don't think this is the strongest deterrence for "advanced" strategies, but it serves as a basic gating against some miner mindlessly putting many low value transactions.
And I guess it works under the premise that maintaining a cartel will be hard under real world conditions.

but it couples the mechanism with the ballpark of coinbase rewards at a given time (ie when rewards become negligible compared to potential fee revenue, block capacity will grow faster). Not saying this is good or bad, just an observation
I agree, but when rewards become negligible compared to fees, well its what should happen in a sense.
but generally I think the deterrence is more focused on being non zero than anything.
no strong opinion, was thinking 60
some degree of rigidity makes sense if we wish for this to have effect is what I think
overall the mechanism seems simple and pretty easy to reason about, which is a big advantage in the current short timeframe
and 60 secs might be annoying for the unlucky bunch just at a huge demand spike, but by the time people finish complaining of this, 60secs have already passed.

I think the underlying idea is - this gives some non zero gap for the prisoner dilemma not to immediately kick in, and help miners coordinate early on what is the correct strategy for them - keeping blocksizes small.  Yet at some point pressure becomes big and prisoner dilemma overtakes.
once a miner is already willing to forego the majority of the block reward - I really doubt they care if its 90% or 100%.

Put differently - you worry of a case where a rouge miner includes 300 txs instead of 60 - but ignoring some advanced manipulations (which don't get me wrong, are a thing, but are tough to actually do) - this will only be rational if these transactions are actually in the mempool, and this ain't a problem then since it means we do have high throughput
the reason some penalty is good is to discourage a greedy miner from including more transactions when there is low throughput in the system.
at least that's how I see it, maybe I should dig into what monero had in mind exactly
I do think that the reward should be steep early on, to counteract the natural pressure to just include all txs  you see. but once this point breaks - I'm not sure how much I care of the details.
I'm not talking about manipulation. Even if there's a demand spike it can result in very low fees.

There's also another proposal I discussed with @FreshAir08 in private.
This is a proposal by Meni Rosenfeld that basically says that the block size limit is some bounded monotone function f that depends on the minimal tx fee in a block.

i.e. (I write block size as number of txs)

f(0 kas)=20
f(0.1 kas)=100
f(1 kas)=200
f(infty)=230
This is nice because there's no penalty and it's decoupled from the block reward. but you have to put the correct parameters, and it might be affected by KAS price.
It basically says: you're only allowed to create large blocks if it only contains txs with high fees

@Ross_ku_963

Michael rightfully pointed out doubts in the ability of the monero adaptive blocksize mechanism to truly increase the security budget. Can you respond and  expand on how you see it?

I've been thinking about Michael's point — that the penalty becomes ineffective once coinbase rewards become negligible compared to fees.

  The root of this issue is that FreshAir's proposal is based on Monero's adaptive block size. In Monero, penalizing only the coinbase works fine. But that's because Monero has tail emission — a permanent fixed issuance of 0.6 XMR per block, forever.

  Currently, about 97% of Monero miner income comes from coinbase, with fee accounting for only ~3%. Because coinbase permanently dominates, penalizing coinbase alone covers the vast majority of miner revenue.

  Kaspa has no tail emission. Emission decays toward zero. So:

  - Short term: coinbase >> fees → coinbase penalty is effective
  - Medium term: coinbase ≈ fees → penalty loses half its force
  - Long term: coinbase << fees → penalty becomes nearly meaningless

  Importing Monero's design directly breaks down because the underlying assumption doesn't hold for Kaspa.

  The fix is straightforward: apply the penalty to the combined total of coinbase and fees, not coinbase alone.

  // Monero-style (coinbase only)
  Profit = R_coinbase × (1 - ((W - M) / M)²) + Fees

  // Proposed (combined penalty)
  Profit = (R_coinbase + Fees) × (1 - ((W - M) / M)²)

  This way, the penalty always scales proportionally to total miner income regardless of which component dominates. The mechanism adapts automatically as Kaspa's reward structure shifts over time.
The penalty could maybe be redistributed to the other blocks in the round in inverse proportion to their block size, so there's some incentive to shrink too.

Hi, sorry for intruding here. I was thinking about some interesting applications of covenants such as zk proofs of reserves and utxo-based oracles, and I thought it would be nice to have an opcode allowing to access some recent utxo commitment. Is that feasible or would it break something? Sorry if it has already been discussed, i skimmed and it didn't seem so.
You're aware that the utxo commitment can be used to prove anything only if you provide the full utxo set as preimage?
There's no way to build any succinct witness for partial data
Can't the commitment be a public input and the preimage a private input to the proof? Sorry I'm still learning these things...
Yes it can
I'm just making sure you realize that it's a massive prover effort
It will need to hash and aggregate the full utxo set, with blake2b, chacha20 and muhash ops per entry
Right, I hadn't thought about that :( Indeed it looks like a massive circuit unless some tricks exist.

But we can operate over mergeset utxo diff.

Lets say we have selected parent commitment
And current commitment.

We can only give preimage of mergeset utxo set to prove:
Parent.commitment * diff = current commitment

We can't prove reserves itself, but we can prove it was increased or decreased by x

If we assume we have such proofs for every mergeset since first utxo sent to specific spk - we can prove reserves too

Probably they also have to be settled on L1 to keep preimage relatively small

Does it provide anything significantly different than the seq commit? 
If you wish to prove a new utxo entered the set you can prove it via the accepted txn
Multiplication is faster than calculating of Merkle root for txs. And requires less cycles

To prove utxo change we need to prove utxo commits to txid and txid commits to seq commit.

Last one is performed once. But we need to prove every tx has or hasn't utxo we are looking for
Hmm I don't mind the opcode (it can use the same seqcommit accessor with same chainblock and threshold logic), but the speedup here is doubtful and I'm not sure there's a real usecase
Btw for proof of reserves you only need to provide the utxos you own (+ membership proofs) as a witness, not the full set preimage, am I wrong?
There's no membership proof(that doesn't require the whole set)
I mean merkle path
I got you. There's no Merkle tree for utxo set. We use muhash
Oooh sorry didn't know that...

We sacrifice membership proofs for faster rolling updates via multiplicative hashing. 
The rationale is that the main usecase is spv/pp proving of the full set

Michael raised good questions on this implementation proposal, one of them was (simplifying, not 1:1 paraphrase): Is there a technical reason this implementation create a new tcp server (listener) and/or could we reuse the existing underlying socket in both contexts

From my current understanding, Overlay (new context: via libp2p) uses lower-level Tcp Stream while Direct (current classic p2p) uses higher abstraction.
I researched a few hours on this, and my conclusion was that, in order to use the same server/socket/listener, we would need to define the server implementation using manual orchestration (still use tonic; but do the wiring ourselves instead of letting the lib doing it for us).
This would allow us to access raw tcp stream and only "upgrade" them when context says so (in direct it's instant, in libp2p a few rounds are necessary, preliminary)

But, it's all still conceptual and a simple PoC would need to be conducted to attest the validity.
Another possible path (which imo is undesirable for several reasons), is to evaluate usage of hyper.

Still some things to be done, especially with diagnostic displays. But we're getting there

Rusty Kaspa v1.1.0 is out (stable production release)

https://github.com/kaspanet/rusty-kaspa/releases/tag/v1.1.0

## Rusty Kaspa v1.1.0

This is a major upgrade over v1.0.1.

The main story in this release is not one feature, but a meaningful step forward across integration UX, sync/catchup behavior, and node efficiency.

Main highlights:

- VSPC v2 (`GetVirtualChainFromBlockV2`): a significant upgrade for integrators. It provides a much cleaner flow for consuming virtual-selected-chain progression together with accepted tx context. It also provides richer tx data (including sender and fee-related use cases), configurable verbosity, and optional confirmation gating. In practice, this removes much of the multi-flow stitching/reconciliation burden from integrators.
- IBD catchup improvements: this release introduces the pruning catchup flow, covering the case where the syncer advances its pruning point while syncing a fresh syncee.
- Performance + storage gains: header multi-level parent data is now compressed across memory, DB, and network paths, and higher-level relation stores are no longer maintained for all headers continuously (they are built on demand only for pruning-proof headers). This cuts always-on overhead, and in practice gives ~3x faster header-stage IBD.

Other notable work:
- RocksDB presets for HDD/archive-oriented setups, WAL directory support and cache controls
- in-house Rusty Kaspa Stratum Bridge released as BETA
- continued Crescendo-related cleanup/hardening across consensus and infrastructure layers

Includes DB schema/version upgrades. Upgrading from older DB versions is supported, but downgrading later may require DB deletion.

Overall, v1.1.0 is a stronger production baseline: easier to integrate, lighter/faster in important paths, and more stable in sync/pruning-heavy scenarios. It also matters for what comes next: it gives us a cleaner/stabler/more performant base before the upcoming covenants++ releases.

Hello everyone :)

i have a few brainstorming about covenant usability over its lifetime.

right now, I can take a .sil (or a handcrafted opcode list), parameterize it, for example with max_supply = 1337, and deploy it through a transaction output with a computed covenant_id.

at that point, only I know the covenant state, while observers can only see that a covenant was deployed.

my first question was: if I want this state to be publicly available, how should it be shared with the network?
@someone235 suggested an idea: an opt-in convention where the state (+more) is included in the transaction payload. That also seems useful for inter-connected covenants.
so with that, deployment, state sharing, and execution all seem covered.

the remaining question is: how do we preserve state past pruning?
one option would be an indexer storing near-full (what's necessary to hash it) transaction data, so historical covenant data remains available and verifiable after pruning.

would it make sense to embed such an indexer into kaspad, enabled via an optional CLI flag?
important point: ideally, historical data should be retrievable trustlessly from another node.
@someone235 Feel free to fix me if I mis-interpreted something that came from you

Fixes the recording and display of arguments at the contract level (previously ctors were called consts, and recent PR added a new constant declaratio. Now there is a separation between them in the recording and the cli displaying them nicely together)

New debugging feature - eval <expr> 


(sdb) e this.activeInputIndex
this.activeInputIndex = (int) 0
(sdb)  e OpSha256(1)
OpSha256(1) = (byte[]) [0x4b, ...
(sdb) e BONUS + delta / 2
BONUS + delta / 2 = (int) 7
(sdb)
Nice, being able to eval is pretty neat!

opened https://github.com/kaspanet/rusty-kaspa/pull/919 which resolves an issue consumers of some rk crates are hitting depending on which transitive dep (js-sys) version resolves to (default: fails)
workaround in the meantime, manually edit cargo.lock and pin js-sys to 0.3.77
edit: despites pr title and ambiguous description, this issue affects anyone using kaspa-wallet-core for example, in a new project (or regenerating the cargo lockfile)
